We have moved from 'From Russia with love' to

From Ukraine with "love"

quotedTweet.text:
So we keep talking about Ukrainian drones attacking Russian refineries. 

Pulling out my Refinery Configuration Schematic, I see 3 big scenarios:

1⃣If the drones struck the CDU (the crosses in blue), there's refinery downtime and the immediate hit is on distillates production and more crude exports.

2⃣If the drones struck the reformer (the small yellow crosses), you are going to get less mogas but more naphtha exports from Russia. 

3⃣If the drones struck the important secondary units like the FCC and HCU (the big red crosses), you are going to get less mogas and diesel respectively, and more fuel oil exports from Russia (if the CDU is still running). 

Also it usually takes a longer time to repair damaged secondary units vs the CDU due to more sophisticated parts.

I don't have visibility of where the strikes happened, but we can see European product cracks for direction.
 
#oott

text:
Iran is making another push to direct all Hormuz traffic to its shipping lanes, away from the Omani side. It has fired to several vessels this morning. 

Part of its plan seems, too, allowing multiple Japanese ships to cross: yesterday 6 Japanese oil tankers crossed, and 2 more are crossing today.

text: HEDGE FUNDS and other money managers are much more bullish about the outlook for the price of gasoline than diesel or crude oil. U.S. gasoline inventories were severely depleted during the war between the United States and Iran as refiners prioritised production of more jet fuel. Fund managers have boosted their net position in U.S. gasoline futures and options to a 14-week high equivalent to 72 million barrels (78th percentile for all weeks since 2011). The net position is far more bullish than for diesel (52 million barrels, 43rd percentile) or crude (141 million barrels, 1st percentile):
Gone now.

quotedTweet.text: Following the exodus of Japanese tankers, there are very few stranded tankers left.

text:
The gap between empty tanker entries into the Persian Gulf and the volume of fresh loadings is closing, thanks to:

1) improved loadings, especially from Saudi Arabia

2) a pullback in the pace of empty tanker entries

U.S. HOUSE DEMOCRATS MOVE TO BLOCK TURKEY JET ENGINE SALE

A group of House Democrats introduced a resolution to stop the sale of advanced U.S. jet engines to Turkey, challenging the Trump administration’s push to move forward despite existing legal restrictions on military sales to Ankara.

Arabica #coffee futures surged as much as 19% on Monday, the steepest one-day rise in 26 years, as El Niño-related weather concerns extended a one-month rally that has lifted prices by 45%. Widespread expectations of a bumper crop in top producer Brazil have been tempered by poor weather that has delayed harvesting, prompting some growers to hold back sales in anticipation of higher prices. Meanwhile, #cocoa futures jumped 13% to their highest since January as persistent rains across key West African growing regions added to supply concerns, squeezing funds holding sizeable short positions.

7.7.26

AI & SEMICONDUCTOR SHOCK
- Samsung Earnings Miss Concerns: Samsung Electronics flagged a 19-fold jump in Q2 operating profit, surpassing its combined earnings over the past three years. However, its shares slid as a slight revenue miss—driven by more moderate DRAM price hikes than anticipated—failed to ease deep structural concerns over the durability of the AI infrastructure boom. The KOSPI index fell as much as 5% on the day.
- Memory Backlash & Rotations: The results triggered heavy profit-taking across global memory names, driving an MSCI Asia technology index down 4.9%. Investors rotated heavily into consumer, financial, and communication sectors. A critical market test looms Friday when South Korea's SK Hynix begins trading its $28 billion ADR package on the Nasdaq.
- Mega-Capex Projections: Despite immediate jitters, consensus forecasts from Visible Alpha show combined Q2 capital spending by Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta surging 74% YoY to hit $168 billion, validating near-term hardware allocations.
- Sun Valley M&A Focus: The annual Allen & Co. Sun Valley conference kicks off today in Idaho. The guest list features top AI figures including OpenAI's Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, and Anthropic's Dario Amodei, turning focus toward potential consolidation and strategic partnerships.

SPACEX NASDAQ-100 INCLUSION
- Passive Inflow Day: SpaceX (SPCX) officially joins the Nasdaq-100 index today. Despite its $2.1 trillion market cap, the rocket and AI giant will carry an initial weight of less than 1% due to Nasdaq's free-float adjustments (SpaceX sold less than 5% of its shares in June's primary offering). Indexers hoarding stock at Monday's close drove an aggressive volume spike.
- Underwriter Coverage Unleashed: The industry-mandated quiet period expired today for underwriting syndicates. At least six major institutions initiated coverage with top-tier buy recommendations. Morgan Stanley labeled the firm "AI's final frontier," while Goldman Sachs, BofA, Citi, and UBS issued highly bullish structural targets focused on Starlink data scalability and Starship reusability. 
- Bank Earnings Tailwind: This historic capital market issuance, coupled with robust M&A advisory fees, will power Q2 sales and trading divisions at major Wall Street banks. JPMorgan, Bank of America, Citigroup, Wells Fargo, and Goldman Sachs report on July 14, with Morgan Stanley following on July 15.

GEOPOLITICS, NATO SUMMIT & ENERGY
- Tanker Attacks in Hormuz: Two commercial vessels were struck overnight in the Strait of Hormuz, fracturing the fragile truce. Fired upon by Iranian forces, the Qatari LNG tanker Al Rekayyat suffered an engine room fire, while a Saudi crude oil supertanker reported localized hull damage. 
- Deadlock and Warnings: Iran's Foreign Ministry announced a complete suspension of peace talks unless President Trump halts his threats to resume full military action. WTI crude remains heavily capped below $70/bbl, though a minor squeeze is developing on short stops.
- NATO Arms Deals: Ahead of a highly charged summit with President Trump, NATO leaders in Turkey unveiled tens of billions in arms agreements. European nations announced major surveillance drone orders from Northrop Grumman (NOC) and defensive aircraft buys from Sweden's Saab, while the US is negotiating domestic European missile production lines to secure Ukraine supply. EU Natural Gas surged 4% on the news.

MACROECONOMICS, RATES & FX
- Extreme Dollar Longs: Global speculators have accumulated a massive $40 billion net-long dollar position—the highest in more than a decade—according to CFTC data released July 6. The greenback’s 2% surge in June was heavily driven by newly sworn-in Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's vow to restore price stability. Markets are currently pricing in roughly 1.24 quarter-point rate hikes through year-end.
- FOMC Minutes Loom: Traders look to Wednesday's release of the June FOMC minutes for technical insights into how Warsh is structuring forward guidance and managing consensus among the nine members who plotted near-term rate hikes before the crude crash.
- Yen Short Cap: Hedge funds turned the most net-short on the yen since 2007. Wide interest-rate differentials continue to fuel yen carry trades into higher-yielding dollars, pushing USD/JPY to a 40-year high of 162.82 as Japanese authorities refrain from active intervention.

FINANCIAL SERVICES REGULATION (DEBIT CARDS)
- Bypassing the Durbin Amendment: Large financial institutions are exploring network acquisitions to circumvent Dodd-Frank limits on debit-card transaction fees. Capital One's $50.6B Discover acquisition highlighted that owning the underlying routing infrastructure provides an exemption from federal interchange caps.
- Fiserv Moves: Attention is turning to financial technology providers that connect Wall Street to retail merchant systems. Fiserv (FISV), which owns the STAR and Accel processing networks, jumped 5% this morning after losing 70% of its market value over the past year.

AGRICULTURE, EL NIÑO & WEATHER SHOCKS
- Cocoa and Coffee Spikes: Cocoa futures in New York surged to their highest absolute level since January due to heavy rainfall in the Ivory Coast and Ghana, raising black pod disease risks. 
- Harvest Delays: Concurrently, unseasonal rainfall in Brazil has severely delayed the local coffee harvest, causing growers to hoard inventory. September coffee futures surged 20% over the last week. Forecasters warn a strengthening El Niño cycle is increasing severe weather and flooding across the Florida peninsula.

CALENDAR
- Tue (Today): 2026 Sun Valley Conference begins. Japan 30Y Treasury Auction, Labor Cash Earnings. EU German Industrial Production. BOE Financial Stability Report. US Trade Balance, JOLTS Job Openings, Redbook Retail Sales. Treasury $58B 3-Year Note Auction. API Weekly Petroleum Report.
- Wed: Germany Wholesale Price Index. UK REC Report on Jobs. US MBA Mortgage Applications, Wholesale Inventories. June FOMC Policy Meeting Minutes (2:00 p.m. ET). US Consumer Credit. DOE Crude and Distillate Inventories. $39B 10-Year Note Auction. Trump meets Erdogan/NATO Leaders in Turkey.
- Thu: China June CPI & PPI. Japan Machine Tool Orders. ECB Account of June Meeting (7:30 a.m. ET). US Weekly Jobless Claims, Existing Home Sales. $22B 30-Year Bond Auction. Fed's Williams, Fed's Logan speak. SK Hynix Final IPO Pricing.
- Fri: Japan PPI. German CPI. Canadian Net Change in Employment. Baker Hughes Rig Count. CFTC Commitment of Traders (3:30 p.m. ET). Fed H8 Bank Balance Sheet Update. SK Hynix Nasdaq trading debut.

Aluminum repricing lower: Goldman cut Q4’26 LME forecast to $2,950/t from $3,200/t as supply recovery erodes the deficit thesis.

2026 deficit estimate drops to 100k tonnes from 720k; 2027 shifts to a ~1.5m tonne surplus with forecast reduced to $2,700/t.

LME aluminum near $3,265/t is testing the $3,254.50 52-week low, while Japan Q3 premiums at $395/t keep the physical tightness debate alive.

Watch $3,254.50 support vs 342/345-350 resistance; crude remains a macro drag with WTI $68.30 (-0.57%) and Brent $71.68 (-0.61%) after OPEC+ supply increases.

MARKET UPDATE — MONDAY, JULY 6, 2026

Markets are starting the week with a strange mix:

🔸 tech futures are bouncing
🔸 oil is falling
🔸 bond yields remain stubborn
🔸 and the Japanese yen is flashing risk warnings again

This is not a simple “risk-on” day.
It is a market trying to celebrate lower inflation pressure while quietly asking whether the AI trade has become too crowded.

🟢 MARKETS: TECH LEADS THE REBOUND

US futures are higher after the July 4 holiday weekend.

S&P 500 futures: around +0.4%
Nasdaq 100 futures: around +1.2%
Dow futures: slightly negative / broadly flat
VIX: around 16.4

The Nasdaq is leading because chip stocks are rebounding after last week’s sharp sell-off.

Intel and Micron rose premarket after heavy losses in semiconductors last Thursday.

Simple explanation:
When chip stocks rise, the Nasdaq usually rises because AI and semiconductor companies are now some of the biggest engines of the US stock market.

But this rebound needs confirmation.
The semiconductor sector has already had a huge run this year, and investors are now asking whether future profits can justify the prices.

🟢 BONDS & FED: THE MARKET STILL FEARS HIGHER RATES

Treasury yields are slightly lower this morning:

1-year yield: around 3.94%
2-year yield: around 4.11%
10-year yield: around 4.46%
30-year yield: around 4.97%

The key point is this:
Yields are not collapsing, even though oil has fallen and jobs data recently weakened.
That matters.

Simple explanation:

Bond yields are like the interest rate the US government has to pay to borrow money.

When yields stay high, it tells us investors still think inflation or tight Fed policy may remain a problem.

The market is now waiting for Wednesday’s Fed minutes from the first meeting under Chair Kevin Warsh.

Traders want to know whether the Fed is preparing to stay tough on inflation — or whether weaker jobs and cheaper oil will reduce pressure for another rate hike.

This week also brings a 10-year Treasury auction on Wednesday and a 30-year auction on Thursday.

That matters because the stock market is trying to rally while bond yields remain stubbornly high.

The market is now waiting for Wednesday’s Fed minutes from the first meeting under Chair Kevin Warsh.

Traders want to know whether the Fed is preparing to stay tough on inflation — or whether weaker jobs and cheaper oil will reduce pressure for another rate hike.

This week also brings a 10-year Treasury auction on Wednesday and a 30-year auction on Thursday.

That matters because the stock market is trying to rally while bond yields remain stubbornly high.

Traders are also watching today’s final S&P Global services and composite PMI readings.

Forecasts point to continued expansion, with the services PMI expected at 51.4 and the composite PMI expected at 52.2.

Simple explanation:

A PMI reading above 50 usually means business activity is still growing.

🟢 OIL: FROM HORMUZ PANIC TO SUPPLY GLUT FEARS

Oil continues to fall.

WTI crude: around $68–69
Brent crude: around $71–72

OPEC+ has agreed to raise production by 188,000 barrels per day in August.

That is the fifth consecutive monthly increase.

This comes as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has improved after the US-Iran 60-day ceasefire.

Weekly crude oil tanker crossings have rebounded to about 57 (up from a wartime average of 15). However, this is still significantly lower than pre-war baseline, which was was roughly 230 to 240 crude oil tanker crossings per week.

Simple explanation:

A few months ago, markets feared the world might not get enough oil.

Now the worry is moving in the opposite direction: too much supply, weaker demand, and lower prices.

That is why some banks now see Brent potentially falling towards $60–65 by year-end if Hormuz keeps reopening and supply continues to return.

For ordinary people, cheaper oil can mean lower petrol and transport costs.

For markets, cheaper oil can reduce inflation pressure.
For energy stocks, however, lower oil can hurt profits.

🟢 AI & TECH: A HUGE WEEK FOR THE AI TRADE

This week could test investor appetite for AI again.

Three major events matter:

🔸Samsung is expected to give a second-quarter update tomorrow.

🔸SK Hynix is preparing an approximately $28bn US listing, one of the largest share sales in history.

🔸SpaceX officially joins the Nasdaq-100 tomorrow.

South Korea is also becoming a warning sign for AI-related speculation.

The KOSPI has surged dramatically over the past year, but volatility has been extreme and foreign investors have been pulling money out.

That matters because Samsung and SK Hynix are now central to the global AI supply chain.

SpaceX’s inclusion matters because funds tracking the Nasdaq-100 may need to buy the stock automatically.

Simple explanation:

When a company joins a major index, some funds have to buy it because their job is to copy that index.

That can create short-term demand.

But it does not guarantee long-term gains.

The real question is still whether earnings, margins and cash flows can support today’s valuations.

🟢 THE EARNINGS BUBBLE QUESTION

The next major test is earnings season.

Investors will be watching whether AI spending by big technology companies is producing enough revenue growth to justify today’s valuations.

In simple terms:

The market has already priced in a lot of good news. Now companies need to prove the profits are real.

This is the biggest quiet risk beneath the surface.

Wall Street analysts now expect very strong S&P 500 profit growth, helped by AI, chips and resilient consumers.

But some investors worry expectations have risen too fast.

The danger is not that AI is fake.

The danger is that markets may be pricing AI profits as if everything goes perfectly.

Simple explanation:

Imagine a student who is expected to score 99% in every exam.

Even if they score 90%, that may still be excellent — but markets may treat it as disappointment.

That is the risk with AI stocks now.

Great companies can still fall if expectations become unrealistic.

🟢 SMALL CAPS: A CONTRARIAN SIGNAL

The Russell 2000 has had a strong year, but investor flows into small-cap funds remain low by historical standards.

That is interesting because neglected areas of the market can sometimes become attractive when too much attention is focussed on mega-cap tech.

Simple explanation:

If everyone is crowded into the same few giant companies, smaller companies can be ignored.

Sometimes that creates opportunity.

But small caps usually need falling rates, stable growth and easier credit conditions to perform well.

🟢 CURRENCIES: THE YEN WARNING RETURNS

The dollar is stronger today.

The yen remains weak, with Goldman Sachs reportedly raising its 12-month USD/JPY forecast to 165.

This matters because a weak yen can encourage the carry trade.

That means investors borrow cheaply in yen and invest in higher-returning global assets.

Simple explanation:

It is like borrowing from a friend who charges almost no interest, then using that money to chase bigger returns elsewhere.

It feels easy while the yen stays weak.

But if the yen suddenly strengthens, traders may have to unwind those positions quickly.

That can force selling across stocks, bonds and other risk assets.

Japan may intervene again if the yen weakens too far, but intervention usually gives temporary relief unless the interest-rate gap changes.

🟢 CORPORATE DEAL FLOW

Corporate activity is also picking up.

🔸Comcast’s Sky is buying ITV’s TV business in a deal reported around the 🔸–$2bn range.

🔸EasyJet shares rose after a takeover approach from Castlelake.

These are not the main market stories, but they show that dealmaking is still alive despite high rates and uncertain growth.

🟢 STOCKS TO WATCH TODAY

Several individual stocks are also in focus:

🔸 Intel and Micron — rising premarket as chip stocks try to rebound after last week’s sharp sell-off.

🔸 easyJet — jumped in London after a takeover approach from Castlelake.

🔸 Comcast / ITV — Comcast’s Sky is buying ITV’s TV business in a deal reported around $1.6bn–$2bn.

Simple explanation:

These are not the main market driver, but they show where traders may see extra movement today.

🟢 WHAT IT MEANS

Today’s market is being pulled in two directions.

Bullish side:

🔸 Oil is falling
🔸 Inflation pressure may ease
🔸 Tech is rebounding
🔸 AI demand remains powerful
🔸 Earnings expectations are strong

Bearish side:

🔸 Bond yields remain high
🔸 Fed minutes could sound hawkish
🔸 AI expectations may be too stretched
🔸 The yen carry trade is a hidden risk
🔸 Oil’s fall could also signal weaker demand

The market is fragile.

The main risk is that investors are assuming three things at once:

🔸inflation falls

🔸earnings surge

🔸AI keeps delivering

If all three happen, the rally can continue.

If one fails, volatility may return quickly.

Thought for the Day:
“The market does not punish optimism. It punishes optimism without a margin of safety.”

Sources: 
MarketWatch, WSJ, Barron’s, Reuters, Financial Times, Bloomberg, Goldman Sachs, TradingView

#StockMarket #AIStocks #BondYields

7 月 7 日 A 股早盘完整观点解读

外围全线上涨 + 三星业绩炸裂

         昨晚美股、中概集体收红，叠加三星电子二季度利润同比暴涨 19 倍、存储周期逻辑再强化，先理清外围利好与隐藏压制风险，再拆解今日大盘节奏、板块分层策略与核心风险。

一、大盘整体节奏：小幅高开震荡，仅情绪修复、非趋势反转

1、开盘驱动：美股三大指数、中概金龙指数全线上涨，费城半导体收涨，叠加三星超预期存储业绩，今日 A 股小幅高开是大概率；
2、三是港股存在大额解禁抛压，北向资金日内分歧加大，高位科技股容易出现高开兑现；三重压制反弹高度的利空，决定反弹空间有限：一是美国财政部流出报告草案，直接警示 AI 赛道存在泡沫风险，类比 2000 年互联网泡沫，压制全球科技股估值预期；二是韩国股市情绪脆弱，三星业绩公布后韩股高开跳水，存储板块冲高回落，拖累全球半导体情绪；
3、操作纪律：今日冲高缩量就是减仓窗口，不开新仓、不追加仓位。当前行情只是外围利好带来的脉冲式情绪修复，不存在单边反转基础；
4、真正趋势拐点需等待 7 月中旬关键数据与会议：7 月 17/21 日国内 GDP 经济数据落地，叠加重磅政策会议定调，在此之前市场整体维持箱体震荡格局。

二、板块强弱分层：执行杠铃配置策略，进攻短线逢高兑现、防御做底仓对冲

（一）进攻短线主线（仅博弈日内修复，不追高，冲高分批减仓）

存储芯片（今日核心主线，催化最密集）
         覆盖标的：江波龙、兆易创新、佰维存储、深科技，同时延伸 HBM 先进封装长电科技、盛合晶微、配套材料华海诚科。
         多重利好共振：三星二季度利润暴涨核心驱动力是 HBM、DRAM 涨价；SK 海力士启动 280 亿美元美股 ADR 上市；隔夜美股存储板块全线走高；国内 SSD 终端产品价格翻倍上涨，行业供需缺口至少延续至 2027 年。

半导体设备、算力硬件
         博通、AMD 隔夜大涨带动板块情绪，但中长期存在利空约束：英伟达 Rubin 全液冷架构交付延期，限制算力硬件中长期上行空间，仅适合短线日内博弈，不做中线持仓。标的：中微公司、北方华创、紫光股份、中际旭创。

新能源车、传媒游戏
         特斯拉、蔚来、B 站中概股昨夜大幅反弹，整车、游戏板块跟随情绪脉冲，属于纯事件驱动反弹，无新增产业催化，持续性偏弱。

人形机器人
         高盛发布行业测算，2035 年全球人形机器人出货量达 140 万台，十年增长 70 倍，叠加宇树科技 IPO 催化，仅题材脉冲行情，高位标的谨慎追涨。标的：三花智控、先锋电子、晋拓股份。

（二）防御底仓赛道（杠铃配置另一端，平滑科技板块高波动）

创新药、中药
隔夜美股减肥药赛道集体大跌，资金高低切换逻辑明确，震荡市中药、CXO 能稳定账户回撤，作为中长期均衡底仓持有。标的：药明康德、首药控股、康恩贝。

必选消费、养殖、高股息
传统防御板块，对冲科技高位回调风险，适合稳健投资者配置，包含生猪养殖、食品饮料、电力煤炭高股息蓝筹。

航空、化工化纤
国际原油大幅降价，上游原材料成本持续改善，板块业绩确定性稳定，具备波段机会。

三、本周关键风险提示（周三重点盯防）

本周周三将公布美联储议息会议纪要，若纪要措辞偏鹰、释放收紧流动性信号，全球高估值 AI 成长赛道会再度承压，高位存储、光模块、机器人标的回调风险会显著放大。

四、整体操作总结

仓位控制：维持现有仓位，今日不新开仓、不加仓，冲高缩量主动减仓高位科技；
交易思路：进攻板块只做日内低吸博弈反弹，绝不追高开；防御板块底仓不动，平衡账户波动；
中期等待：所有重仓加仓动作，统一延后至 7 月中旬 GDP 数据、重磅会议落地后再判断。

🌅 StockKit Morning Report — Tuesday, July 7, 2026
🌙 Overnight Futures
S&P -0.2% · Dow +0.3% · Nasdaq -1%
 — 
Nasdaq-100 futures fell Tuesday, weighed down by a decline in chip stocks as investors once again appear to rotate out of names tied to artificial intelligence.
📈 Yesterday's Close
Dow +0.29% (record close 53,055.91) · S&P +0.72% (7,537.43) · Nasdaq +1.12% (26,121.16)
 — 
US stocks rose on Monday as pressure on the tech sector eased, with chip stocks shaking off a recent dip.
 
Big Tech players like Alphabet, Apple, Meta, and Tesla rallied.
⚠️ The Story Continues
The downward pressure began in Asia-Pacific markets, after South Korea's Kospi dropped nearly 5% following a nearly 7% drop in memory chipmaker Samsung Electronics.
 
The company reported a big jump in second-quarter profit, though concerns about spending and demand overshadowed the increase.
 
Micron was last seen 5% lower in premarket trading, with KLA, Marvell Technology, Broadcom and AMD also posting declines.
SpaceX prepared for its Nasdaq-100 debut, and investors awaited the Fed minutes for clues on the interest-rate outlook.
 
The June FOMC minutes drop at 2:00 p.m. ET tomorrow (Wednesday, July 8).
 
The FOMC has held rates at 3.5%–3.75% for four consecutive meetings, and the June dot plot now leans toward at least one rate hike rather than a cut — a meaningful change in tone from earlier in the year.
Shell hiked its integrated gas production guidance on Tuesday, but said output would still be markedly lower than it was in the first quarter due to the impact of the U.S.-Iran war.
 
WTI crude futures are hovering near $69 a barrel following reports of easing supply constraints in the Strait of Hormuz.
 
Weak June jobs data (57,000 added vs. 110,000 expected) plus downward revisions to prior months took some heat off the hike narrative — the probability of a July hike dropped from around 29% to about 18%.
📅 Today
72 Economic Events scheduled
 · 
SpaceX (SPCX) officially joins the Nasdaq-100
 · 
No noteworthy earnings reports scheduled before the bell.
 Later this week: 
PEP (Thursday), DAL (Friday)
⚠️ Bottom Line
The AI rotation continues — Samsung's soft guidance just re-ignited chip anxiety. Expect semis to test support early. The broader market remains constructive: Dow at record highs, labor market cooling, oil off the boil. But watch that Fed minutes release tomorrow — Warsh's hawkish lean is real and the dot plot shift matters. Today favors defense over offense. If you're adding, look for names trading below Consensus Analyst Price Targets in sectors less tied to AI mania. Let the tape settle before chasing yesterday's winners.
Always check the news before you buy. Check the Consensus Analyst Price Target — prefer stocks trading below it. The decision to buy is yours.
Despite the global news, Happy Trading!

Key Points Summary
Nasdaq futures lower: September Nasdaq 100 E-Mini futures are down 0.97%, led by a selloff in semiconductor stocks.
Samsung disappoints despite strong earnings: Samsung forecast a 19-fold increase in Q2 operating profit, but investors expected even more. Its shares fell 6.9%, dragging global chip stocks lower. U.S. chipmakers Sandisk and Micron dropped over 5% premarket.
Oil rises: WTI crude gained nearly 1% after attacks on shipping near the Strait of Hormuz raised supply concerns.
Treasury yields increase: The 10-year Treasury yield climbed to 4.50% as markets priced in a greater chance of tighter Fed policy.
Monday's U.S. market: Stocks finished higher, led by AI and chip names:Western Digital: +7%
AMD: +6%
Dell: +4% after positive comments from President Trump.
Broadcom: +3% on an expanded Apple chip partnership through 2031.

Economic data: U.S. services activity continued expanding in June but slowed slightly. Inflation pressures eased somewhat, while the employment component improved.
Federal Reserve:Governor Christopher Waller defended using forward guidance.
Markets currently price a 75% chance of no rate change and a 25% chance of a 25 bp hike at the next Fed meeting.

Today's focus:U.S. Trade Balance report (expected deficit: -$78.3B).
Remarks from Fed Vice Chair Michelle Bowman.

Europe: Stocks fell, led by technology shares. Germany's industrial production beat expectations (+0.9% vs. +0.1% expected), providing some support.
Asia: Markets were broadly weaker:China: Property stocks declined amid weaker growth outlook from the World Bank.
Japan: Tech stocks led losses as investors questioned AI-sector valuations despite strong Samsung earnings.

Notable premarket movers:Down: Sandisk (-6%), Marvell (-4%), Rivian (-8%), SpaceX (-1%).
Up: Microsoft (+1%), Amazon (+1%), Fiserv (+6%) on reports it may sell its debit network.

Overall Market Theme
The dominant story is renewed weakness in semiconductor stocks, outweighing generally positive economic data and yesterday's market gains. Investors are also watching Fed policy expectations, oil prices, and upcoming U.S. trade data for further market direction.

Welcome to this week’s Fortune Gulf Brief. We’ll be covering:  

  • Gulf oil flows return as market eyes potential glut  
  • Mubadala unlocks $25 billion credit portfolio for outside investors 
  • U.S.’ Lux Capital leads Gulf’s $30 million AI funding round 
  • Saudi courts China amid strained U.S. relations 

OPEC+ has agreed to raise oil production by a further 188,000 barrels per day from August, marking the fifth consecutive monthly increase in output quotas as the group continues to unwind its earlier production cuts. 

That brings the total increase in output quotas to around 940,000 barrels a day since the war began. 

The move comes as oil prices continue to ease amid Gulf states ramping up production and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz calming fears of major supply disruptions.  

Brent crude is now trading around $72 per barrel, down from its April peak of $126 per barrel and close to pre-conflict levels. 

Saudi Arabia, the world’s top exporter, shipped an average of 6.3 million barrels a day last week, restoring flows to almost 90% of February’s pre-war levels. 

Meanwhile, UAE oil exports have now overtaken pre-war levels, according to data compiled by energy intelligence company Kpler. 

The country, which formally exited OPEC+ on May 1, shipped 3.94 million barrels a day of crude and condensate in June.  

In addition to ramping up its production since leaving OPEC+, Kpler senior oil analyst Johannes Raubal said the UAE has also been drawing down crude inventories, further enhancing export volumes. 

But the surge in supply is beginning to raise concerns. Analysts at Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs warned last week that the market could be heading for a glut next year if producers continue pumping without consideration of demand. 

China, the world’s largest oil importer, remains one of the biggest question marks.  

The Middle East typically accounts for around half of China’s crude oil imports, but shipments declined in April to their lowest level in almost a decade, according to Kpler data. 

Despite cutting imports by roughly 5 million barrels a day compared with pre-war levels, it has yet to significantly increase its buying. 

Meanwhile, more than 60 million barrels of oil that were effectively stranded when the war broke out have now been released onto the market, following the signing of the U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding, Bloomberg reported last week. 

It noted that UAE oil is traveling as far afield as the U.S. and is even being offered to buyers in Hawaii. 

Melissa Hancock

Chart of the Day: $CL (Crude Oil)

Why this asset today?

Crude oil has bounced from a major support zone and broken out of its lower timeframe range. While the higher timeframe trend remains bearish, the short-term setup favors a pullback to the upside.

Any news or event?

U.S. crude inventories fell by 3.8 million barrels last week, while global supply remains tight as Gulf exports are still below pre-conflict levels. This is supporting oil prices in the short term.

Technical snapshot

• Trend: Bullish (short term)
• Key level: $69
• Support: $66
• Resistance: $78

What to watch for

• Above $69 → Bullish continuation toward $78
• Below $69 → Likely chop or retest of $66

Current view

Bullish in the short term. The lower timeframe breakout and supportive supply data suggest a relief rally, even though the higher timeframe trend remains bearish.

The Speculator’s Glossary Series Topic 46: Fundamental Analysis

Fundamentals aren't textbook GDP growth statistics; they are the "ultimate hands" of real-world supply and demand.
Amateurs think reading an earnings report is analysis; elite speculators harness satellite data to track crude inventories and big data to monitor global port throughput. In the short term, price is driven by sentiment; in the long run, it is dragged back by the iron laws of supply and demand. Trading without fundamentals is driving a supercar at night with your eyes shut.
At Bifu, we integrate top-tier global macro data feeds and real-time financial intel. We strip away media sensationalism to expose the nucleus of asset pricing. Comprehend the underlying supply and demand, and you start running before the tidal wave hits.

One Account, Trade the World.

My trading board has zero scheduled economic data on it today. No central bank, no tier-one release, nothing due. That quietly changes how I read every move.

With no data on the way, whatever moves is positioning and flow, not new information. A break on a no-print day is far easier to fake than one driven by an actual release, so I don't trust it the same and I don't chase it.

$USDCAD is the clean example. It has run from its late-April base near 1.355 up to the June highs and now sits pinned just under 1.42, extended, with no real pullback yet. On a quiet day I have no interest in buying that into the highs. I want it back near its rising 20-day average around 1.41, or a genuine reaction to tomorrow's US crude inventory print, before it earns a trade.

Tomorrow the calendar reloads: an overnight RBNZ decision, that crude number, a 10-year auction. So today the whole job is smaller. Levels marked, powder dry, ready for when the data actually shows up.

🟦 MORNING BRIEFING: While You Slept — July 7, 2026

⭕ DEVELOPING

• Multiple explosions hit Damascus near the hotel hosting French President Emmanuel Macron.

• A Qatari LNG tanker was struck near the Strait of Hormuz and is reportedly at risk of exploding.

• NATO summit opens in Ankara, with Trump, Erdogan, Zelensky and alliance leaders in attendance.

• Iran continues public threats against Trump and Israel following Khamenei’s funeral events.

U.S. —

• President Trump arrived in Ankara for the NATO summit and was greeted by Turkish President Erdogan.

• Trump said he attended the summit because of Erdogan, calling him “a friend” and “a respected leader.”

• Reuters reports Trump is expected to support an F-35 sale to Turkey.

• Trump called for $350 billion in defense funding through Reconciliation 3.0 and passage of the SAVE America Act.

• A U.S. defense official said PAC-3 missile production outside the United States remains an open possibility.

ISRAEL —

• The IDF and Shin Bet eliminated two Hamas commanders in Gaza this week.

• Nukhba commander Ahmed Yahya Ibrahim Batash was killed in northern Gaza.

• Hamas military intelligence commander Hamouda Abu Daqa was killed in southern Gaza.

• IDF paratroopers found an old lathe used to produce rockets in Rafah.

• Israeli forces demolished a terrorist’s home in Nablus.

• Israeli flag raised on Talet Ali al-Taher in southern Lebanon.

• N12 reports Israel has no plans to escalate in Lebanon as long as Hezbollah does not attack IDF forces.

IRAN —

• Foreign Minister Araghchi was met with “Honorless!” chants from a crowd angry over U.S. negotiations.

• Iranian state media implied IRGC responsibility after a Qatari LNG tanker was struck near Hormuz.

• Araghchi warned that final-deal talks cannot begin if threats continue.

• Iran claimed it recovered a 1,000-pound warhead from a U.S. JASSM cruise missile in Kurdistan province.

• At Khamenei’s funeral, mourners hung a Trump effigy from gallows and tore apart a Trump billboard.

• Hassan Nasrallah’s son attended prayer ceremonies in Qom ahead of Khamenei funeral events.

LEBANON —

• The U.S. is pushing to deploy the Lebanese army in Farun, Janduria, and the Zawtar areas near Bint Jbeil.

• A Lebanese source said the move is meant to separate the Lebanon track from Iran and Hezbollah’s demands.

• The framework agreement remains under direct American supervision.

• Israeli media says serious implementation by Israel is expected to begin in the coming days.

EUROPE —

• Macron met Syrian President al-Sharaa in Damascus during the first visit by a major Western leader to post-Assad Syria.

• France says Marine Le Pen remains eligible to run for president despite her 15-month public office ban.

• Germany’s Merz credited talks with Canada and Norway for new trilateral defense agreements.

• The body of Anastasia Brezovska, suspected in the Monaco bombing, was found in Kyiv with gunshot wounds.

• A nationwide blackout hit Cuba after its power grid collapsed.

UKRAINE / RUSSIA —

• Zelensky told NATO that Ukraine’s membership is a “logical step.”

• Ukraine struck eight sanctioned Russian shadow-fleet tankers overnight in the Sea of Azov.

• Ukrainian forces intercepted a Russian ballistic missile over Kyiv with a PAC-3 interceptor.

• The Kremlin said it will closely watch the NATO summit after “confrontational” statements toward Russia.

MIDDLE EAST  —

• Macron said nothing can smother Syrians’ aspiration to live in a sovereign, safe, pluralistic, united Syria.

• Syrian President al-Sharaa said Syria wants France to be its “first partner.”

• Syria said the Hormuz crisis has restored its role as a crossroads for global corridors.

• Syrian and Lebanese officials signed a deal to form a joint high committee for bilateral cooperation.

• A second vehicle exploded near Macron’s hotel in Damascus. Three people were reportedly killed.

• A Saudi-flagged crude tanker was damaged off Oman near the Strait of Hormuz.

GLOBAL —

• NATO launched a counter-drone marketplace and a $40 billion Drone Edge Initiative.

• China’s Huanggang tornado death toll rose to at least 4, with more than 170 injured.

WHAT TO WATCH TODAY

• Whether the Hormuz tanker strikes trigger a U.S. or Gulf response.

• Trump’s NATO meetings with Erdogan, Zelensky and alliance leaders.

• Any escalation after the Damascus explosions near Macron’s hotel.

• Israeli-Lebanese implementation steps under U.S. supervision.

• Iran’s next move after public backlash over U.S. talks.

That's your Morning Briefing.

🛢️ Hormuz Risk Lifts Oil, but Supply Pressure Remains
Brent is trying to rebound as fresh headlines around the Strait of Hormuz bring some risk premium back into the market. Reports of vessel attacks near the route reminded traders that supply risks have not fully disappeared.

Still, the upside is not clean. OPEC+ is raising output again, Saudi Arabia has cut prices sharply for Asian buyers, and Gulf exports are recovering. This keeps the broader supply picture heavy and limits confidence in a strong rally.

For now, Brent looks headline-driven. Any fresh Hormuz or Red Sea escalation can push prices higher quickly, but softer demand signals and extra supply may cap follow-through.

Technically, the trend is still weak. Price remains below the 50-day SMA and MACD is falling, so the broader structure is bearish. But RSI near 28 shows oversold conditions, meaning a short rebound is possible.

The $72 area remains the first key level to watch. A move above it could support a recovery toward $72.40 and $72.80, while failure there may bring pressure back toward $71.60 and then $70.
